questions.text: I feel that it is healthy and right to put my own needs first in most situations.
I sense when someone is being conspicuously silent to make their absence from the conversation felt.
I prefer to find a decision everyone can accept, even if it means compromise.
I think that most emotional reactions to others' suffering are disproportionate to what is actually needed.
I believe that putting myself last is a natural expression of how I value others.
I find that I process the world through sensation first and thought second.
I notice that people sometimes wonder if I'm affected by things, but I simply process them at a lower volume.
I commonly find myself the one editing and finalizing group deliverables.
I believe that objective analysis of distressing events is the discipline that best equips me to weigh short-term compassion against long-term consequences.
I find it natural to give performance feedback without being asked.
I have found that the depth of my concern for others sometimes costs me in terms of my own energy and wellbeing.
I find that having a system for everything frees up mental energy for what matters.
I am inclined to enforce standards even when the group has not explicitly agreed to them.
I let one-on-one conversations flow according to both people's interests.
I believe that curating my own experiences is one of the richest parts of life.
I think that remaining emotionally regulated during exposure to suffering enables the kind of sustained help that emotional involvement cannot provide.
I prefer to act quickly and course-correct rather than try to predict every consequence.
I tend to miss the significance of pauses and silences in conversation.
I notice that my emotional activation comes with distinct physical markers every time.
I time-box my research phase so I don't lose myself in gathering information.
I feel that the moral force of suffering is intensified when I understand the specific ways in which a person has been affected.
I experience excitement as a pleasant glow that enhances my focus rather than disrupting it.
I keep a maintenance schedule for equipment, subscriptions, and recurring needs.
I tend to miss it when someone is signaling that they want to be included.
I sense when a person tells a self-deprecating joke to preempt a criticism they expect.
I find that simplifying my own life so I can be more available to others is deeply rewarding.
I believe that clear, direct speech is a sign of respect.
I declutter only when accumulation actually interferes with what I'm doing.
I tend to prioritize errands for others over my own errands when time is limited.
I address risks as they materialize rather than cataloging them in advance.
I notice that I can move from a difficult situation to a normal one without needing a transition period.
After a heated exchange, my body stays in fight-or-flight mode long after the words have stopped.
I find that the felt weight of others' hardship is what prevents me from becoming complacent about the state of the world.
I prefer the group to decide together when a project is truly complete.
I tend to accept praise graciously and use it as fuel for further personal achievement.
I sense when a person mentions their credentials or background at a socially calculated moment.
